data.title: FEZ: European Equities ETF, Cheap Price And Valuation, Strong Momentum And Returns
data.description:
Tariffs and trade wars have led to significant market turmoil, with the U.S. equity market seeing significant losses in prior months, and a partial recovery these past few weeks. International equities and equity funds have performed much better, seeing significant gains and outperformance. One of the better performers here is the SPDR EURO STOXX 50 ETF (NYSEARCA:FEZ), with double-digit gains and outperformance YTD. European equities also trade with cheaper valuations than their U.S. peers, have comparable growth rates, stronger momentum and lower volatility. Investing in European equities could also serve to diversify an investor's equity portfolio, almost always a wise move. FEZ is a strong investment opportunity, and a buy.

FEZ - Overview and Analysis

Index and Portfolio

FEZ is an equity index ETF, tracking the EURO STOXX 50 index. It invests in the largest European companies within each industry, for a total of 50 stocks. As with most indexes, applicable stocks must also meet a basic set of inclusion criteria, and there are rules and constraints meant to ensure diversification.

FEZ's portfolio is reasonably well-diversified, with investments in most industry sectors.

Compared to the S&P 500, FEZ leans old-economy value sectors, including financials and industrials, being underweight growth, tech, and healthcare. Sector drifts are due to the underlying prevalence of public equities in European markets. Few European tech companies, basically. Such sector leans are quite common for dividend, income, and value ETFs, although FEZ is not (directly) such a fund. Income investors are generally overweight these sectors, which isn't wrong per se, but something to consider.

FEZ provides exposure to the larger European countries, with significant investments in France and Germany, smaller in the Netherlands, Spain, and Italy. Investments in other countries are nominal, almost certainly due to the fund focusing on larger companies, which are rarely headquartered in smaller countries.

FEZ's largest holdings are all well-known European large-caps, including ASML (ASML) and Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMHF). I don't see some of the biggest European beneficiaries of the current trade war, including Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF), up 160% YTD, and ThyssenKrupp (OTCPK:TYEKF) (TKA), up 176%. These are a bit smaller, hence their exclusion.

Valuation and Growth Analysis

FEZ currently trades with a much cheaper valuation relative to the S&P 500, around 30% - 50% cheaper depending on the metric.

International equity valuations are also much lower than U.S. equity valuations, around 33.6% lower on an earnings basis. Valuation gaps are generally much lower, around 18.0%. So, international equities are much cheaper than U.S. equities, and valuation gaps are much wider than average. Regardless of how one looks at the situation, international equities are cheap.

FEZ's cheaper valuation is due to the fact that U.S. equities almost always trade with a premium relative to international, including European, stocks. Said premium is due to the growth and stability offered by the U.S. economy, the size and depth of its public equity markets, and strong medium-term returns (since the 2010s at least).

Importantly, realized and expected growth do not seem to explain the differences in valuation here. FEZ's realized growth seems slightly higher than that of the S&P 500, expected growth slightly lower, although I'm not exactly sure how Morningstar calculates its data. The overall situation is crystal clear, though. Figures for FEZ:

Figures for the S&P 500:

Looking at the broader economic context, European economic growth remains low, although it is expected to improve in the coming years. The ongoing war in Ukraine, trade war with the U.S., and changes to the country's foreign policy, are significant sources of uncertainty and risk for the European Union, but these challenges might just cause the region to wake up. In any case, the challenges by the U.S. seem greater, with surging economic uncertainty, a trade war with the entire world, and worsening economic fundamentals/odds of a recession. In my opinion, the challenges and risks faced by the U.S. are greater, but also somewhat self-inflicted, so easier to solve/unlikely to become fatal.

Performance Track-Record

FEZ's long-term performance track-record is quite mediocre, with the fund significantly underperforming the S&P 500 since inception. Underperformance almost entirely occurred before the pandemic, due to skyrocketing tech prices, earnings, and higher valuations in the same.

On a more positive note, medium-term returns for the fund are much stronger, with FEZ effectively matching (marginally outperforming) the S&P 500 these past five years:

FEZ has also outperformed since my prior articles, slightly since the mid-2023 article, moderately since mid-2024. Medium-term returns look strong, and reasonably consistent.

FEZ's recent outperformance was almost entirely due to the tariffs and ongoing trade war, the expectation of declining U.S. corporate earnings, and changes in investor sentiment (more bullish Europe, more bearish U.S.). Overall, I'm not particularly bearish U.S. myself, but the premium valuations of U.S. equities seem excessive, and mean that these are priced for perfection. Small changes in sentiment can, and have, result in significant underperformance. The same is true for changes in economic or corporate conditions. Declining earnings could, or should, easily have a much bigger impact on U.S. equities than European equities, as growth expectations are much higher in the former (even though growth itself has been similar).

I've mentioned in several of my past articles that cheap international equity valuations should result in some outperformance for these moving forward, but that I didn't really see a catalyst for this. Tariffs seem to have been the catalyst, and the ongoing trade war could result in more underperformance. Guidance has yet to be updated, earnings post-tariffs released, so the situation could worsen still.

Considering the issues above, some international diversification seems wise, and FEZ is a solid choice in this regard.

Dividend Analysis

FEZ sports a 2.6% dividend yield, quite a bit higher than that of the S&P 500, but not particularly high on an absolute basis.

FEZ's slightly higher dividend yield is a small benefit for shareholders, although simply not all that impactful. The higher yield is a reflection of the cheap prices/valuations of European equities, core to their investment thesis.

Conclusion

FEZ provides investors with diversified exposure to large-cap European equities. FEZ's cheap valuation, strong momentum and medium-term returns, make the fund a buy.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Watch The Turnover Of The Monetary Stock
data.description:
I have been making the argument that there is plenty of money around and that this money is available to people to spend the money if they want to.

It's just that the money has not been spent, as the money has reached the private sector.

However, right now, we are on the verge of a situation in which these funds may be put to use, even when the economy looks as if it may be going into a recession.

I have been writing about this possibility now in order to try and make sense of what is happening in the U.S. economy at the present time.

Here is the picture I want to put in front of you.

The M2 money stock totaled $15,416.8 trillion in January 2020, right before the Federal Reserve began shoveling in reserves to the banking system.

A peak was reached in April 2022. M2 money stock totaled $21,749.6 trillion that month.

M2 money stock took a little breather in the following three years but reached another high of $21,762.5 trillion in March 2025.

So, the actions of the Federal Reserve resulted in the money stock rising by just over 41% from January 2020 until April 2022.

Its rise was zero percent from April 2022 to March 2025.

Now, let's take a look at the velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock.

The velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock was 1.39 in the first quarter of 2020.

The velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock was 1.253 in the third quarter of 2022.

And, the velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock was 1.383 in the first quarter of 2025.

In other words, in the first half of this picture, money was being pumped into the U.S. economy to fight the impacts of the Covid-19 pandemic, but it was not being spent at the same pace as just before the economic disruption took place.

By the third quarter of 2022, it appears as if the "velocity" of spending the money stock was returning toward previous levels.

However, by the first quarter of 2025, velocity had returned to roughly where it was before the 2020 recession began.

In other words, the Federal Reserve was pumping lots and lots of money into the economy during the early part of this period.

Americans, however, were not spending it at the same pace they had earlier.

Now, let me take a shot at looking at what the situation was before the first quarter of 2020.

Note that during all of this time, until the collapse of the velocity of circulation just before the 2020 recession, the velocity of circulation was declining, but at a pretty regular rate.

What one could say about this was that the “turnover” of the M2 money stock was declining but at a calm, relatively modest pace.

What was happening here?

Well, in the 2006-2008 period, we had Ben Bernanke, Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System "fussing around" with how monetary policy was connected.

Bernanke was changing monetary policy to the extent that the Fed's primary focus of conducting monetary policy was the quantity of securities the Fed held in its securities' portfolio.

Here is the picture of the securities held outright in the Federal Reserve securities' portfolio.

The Federal Reserve, under the leadership of Ben Bernanke, added securities to the Fed's securities portfolio at a regular, steady pace during this period of time.

The steadiness and regularity of this "quantitative easing" seemed to result in economic units turning over their money balances at a slower and slower pace than what they once did.

The performance of the Federal Reserve changed...and, then, the performance of American economic units changed.

We experienced a structural change in economic activity.

This change resulted in the velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock declining.

And, this explanation helps to show why there was a buildup of monetary balances during the early part of the 2020s.

Cash balances increased all over the place...in financial institutions...in the hands of consumers...in the hands of business people...all across the economy.

But that money is still available for people to use in the near future as the economy works its way out of the Trump planning schedule, the financial needs of the public, and the response of the rest of the world to these things.

I have written several posts over the past few months trying to get people to see that even if the Federal Reserve continues it current round of quantitative tightening...and, even if the federal government continues its excessive budget imbalances...and, even if the whole Trump tariff program generates inflationary pressures...the United States financial system seems to have lots and lots of cash sitting in vaults, available for spending to continue on for some time.

I have been explaining the reason why the Federal Reserve needs to continue to maintain its program of quantitative tightening in order to reduce all the “excess” left over from the Fed's efforts to combat the "Covid-19 crisis."

I have been explaining why I believe that there is plenty of money out in the financial system to finance the Trump tariff system and all that accompanies it.

And, I have been explaining why I believe that the economy is still as strong as it is.

I have been trying to explain why "this time is different" because the Trump effort is not starting out with "nothing" in the way of the finances it needs.

In essence, we are now operating in a different monetary environment than the one described in almost all of our economic books on money and banking.

data.title: SPY: Buy In May For V-Recovery, Even If You Go Away
data.description:
I have been going through my equity de-risk process since the 'liberation day." It just occurred to me that the FED has become a big factor for me to lose my sleep, especially when the chairman speaks, the market becomes more volatile. Today is no exception, the market tanked around 11:00 am when the FOMC decision on the rate hit the wires, but there is no surprise for the FED's rate decision (maintaining) and the comments on the US economy (solid), and the same/similar tariff concerns from the Chairman Powell's previous speak. However, when I look at the market sentiment and recent behavior, I have a strong reason to believe that the wall of worry may come to a limit. It is time to buy the S&P 500. SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) has a good chance to recover further and provide a double-digit gain in 2025 from the current level.

SPY S&P 500 Valuation Update

SPY is an ETF trust managed by State Street. It is one of the most well-known ETFs representing the S&P 500 index with AUM of $572.99 B. I often use SPY as the S&P 500 index or a proxy of "the market" itself.

The S&P 500 fell off 19% from its previous all-time high on February 19, 2025, in about 3 weeks. Despite the obvious blame on President Trump's tariff war, I view the stock selloff as a long-overdue correction that is needed sooner rather than later due to the fast run-up of the US equity market in 2024. S&P 500 gained 23% in 2024 following the previous 24% gain in 2023. As a result, the PE ratio of the S&P 500 reached the highest level in 4 years at the beginning of 2025, as shown below.

The drop in P/E caused by the recent sharp correction has much improved the valuation for the S&P 500. On the other hand, the S&P 500 earnings will continue to grow, as evidenced by the Q1 earnings from mega-cap (34% for the top 10 in SPY) and tech companies (accounting for the biggest 32% weight in SPY).

According to FactSet, "S&P 500 expected to report earnings growth of 10% for CY 2025," and the forward P/E Ratio will be 20.2 for 2025.

I believe PE of 20 and change is a fair valuation, and I would buy the S&P 500 for a long-term holding. My reason is quite simple in that if the S&P 500 can continue to grow earnings at 10% in 2025 under the current conditions, 2026 will see better growth when the rate-cut, tax-cut, and deregulation policy tailwinds will be in place and take effect.

V-Shape or W-Shape recovery?

I think the market reached a bottom when SPY registered a sharp 19% decline from peak to trough on April 8. The market has delivered a 9-day historical comeback (since the 1920s) from the bottom.

Many investors may wonder what recovery we can expect. While nobody can tell for sure, I will use two big recoveries in recent history to illustrate some market behavior patterns.

  1. V-shape recovery: The best example of V-shape recovery is perhaps the one during the COVID-19 crash. As shown in the following chart, the market recovered from over 30% drawdown in about 6 months.
  2. W-shape recovery. I use the W-shape to indicate the pattern when the market bottom was retested, and in some cases, new lows could be reached before it went on a full recovery of the losses. The chart below, on the lower part, shows the W-shape case for the 2022 bear market. The low on November 3rd of 2022 was retested but not broken. Notice the market has not fully recovered to the previous high in about 1 year, which is roughly the whole year of 2023.

My opinion is that this time the market recovery could be more like the V-shape, with some "shallow" pullbacks along the way. To me, similar to COVID-19, the tariff is like a shocking event, and with a few escalations to follow. However, the problems are largely fixable, depending on how much effort we want to make after the first major hit. COVID has many uncertainties (I think more than the current tariff war), the bulk of these became "known" unknowns by the market in a few months. I suspect that the tariff cycle will very likely follow a similar path because the top and bottom tariff rates have already been given. I may not be as smart, but Mr. Market will figure it out sooner than we think, as always. Keep in mind, we just had a 19% near-bear market, so we are off to a better start than the last V-shape recovery from over 32% down.

In addition, there are possibilities that the market has yet to price in. For example, what if the FED does a similar trick as in COVID-19, to "suddenly" cut the rate; or the China tariff is dropped to 50%, which seems to be more tolerable by the resilient US economy, based on the impact of the previous tariff on China in Trump's first term? I will continue to worry about the tariff until it has a clear conclusion, but I am more willing to follow the market wisdom.

"De-risk" the FED, and double digital gain is in sight

Recently, I would not sleep well at night when the FED chairman was scheduled to speak the next day. For example, today's (May 7th) FOMC meeting, the market was ultra-volatile at 11:00 am Pacific time when the FED rate decision hit the wire. As shown in the daily chart below, SPY started a wild swing from a rather normal trading condition. SPY reached a low point at 556 in no time, and yet fully recovered in a relatively short time. There were also multiple V-shaped bounces along the way.

What's interesting to note here is that whatever the FED said or caused becomes a forgotten history very quickly. The SPY would have closed at the same "normal" point if we had cut this period of violent price actions out of the picture.

On the surface, the FED caused this unusual intraday volatility/risk today. However, the FED's rate decision and comments today are pretty much everything expected before the meeting. I wonder if we would be better off if the FED would keep a low-key or no comment (for no action), etc. Ironically, both today and last FOMC on March 19, SPY was up at the close despite the intraday volatility.

I believe next time I will sleep better before the next FOMC meeting, regardless. I would "ignore" the FED and just stay with my long convictions. By the way, I added positions in some SPY top holdings, including NVIDIA, Google, and META, today when SPY was at the intraday low shown above.

Obviously, I am on the bullish side for SPY as I discussed the short-term recovery pattern in the previous section. In the longer term, I believe that with all the tariff-centric risks becoming increasingly more "known", now is a very good time to buy SPY. I expect a double-digit gain by the end of the year from the current level. SPY will reach back to the previous all-time high level by climbing up 10% or higher.

The following is a list of potential catalysts that could drive a higher price for SPY

  • FED rate cut, which has to be done to go back to the neutral interest rate level (3.5%)
  • Some key deals for the tariff will be announced. I view any deal as better than the worst that the market seems to have priced in already.
  • Earnings from meg tech will continue to shine in the coming quarters as the mega AI trend will continue to evolve.
  • Consumers' confidence will be boosted by the rate cut and the resilient market itself
  • US recession fear will be cooling down further. As shown below, the betting odds on a US recession in the Polymarket are still over 50%, although they have dropped from the high sixties. The odds could be much lower when the Q1 earnings season ends. The higher confidence in the US economy will be good for the market.

Risk Analysis

Although I anticipated no worse development from the tariff deals. I implied that both sides of the deals would "suck it up." But it is also possible that one of the other sides would continue to fight for longer until one side backs down. This would cause the impact of GDP growth and inflation to worsen, and the 10% SPY gain would not be achieved in 2025. On the other hand, continued degradation of the GDP and US economy will send the market on a downtrend again and possibly reach a true bear market like what we had in 2022.

Closing Thoughts

Cautions warranted, the market does present a good opportunity these days for long-term investors. The uncertainties around the tariff and trade wars may become "known" unknown soon enough by the market. Investors may also be able to take some calculated risks. The market bottom has formed by the price behavior. It looks like a good entry point for SPY, and I expect a double-digit gain at the end of the year from the current level. Investors who want to wait for the low to be retested could consider opening a partial position. Whether it is a V-shape recovery or a W-shape recovery, I believe there are potential strong catalysts that may come out in the coming months that investors may not want to miss out.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Claros Mortgage Trust, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Claros Mortgage Trust, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Bulls Have No Reason Left For Growth
data.description:
The Fed is not going to give money

Last week, I wrote an article expressing my opinion that the Fed will at least tone down its rhetoric at its meeting. Because the U.S. economy showed weakness in the first quarter and the softening rhetoric avoids conflict with Donald Trump.

But the Fed did not meet my expectations. To summarize Powell's statement after the Fed meeting, we can briefly say that the Fed will not be in a hurry to ease policy. And that the Fed believes that higher duties and the resulting higher prices because of it will alienate the prospects for policy easing.

And here we can remember that almost a year ago, the Fed began to reduce rates. But then the situation in the economy was more favorable than today. But back then, in my opinion, the Fed was friends with the White House! And today, there is no friendship, and Powell preferred to show character, and in my opinion, clearly indicated that the Fed is not going to help Trump.

Conflict with Trump

The current position of the Fed, in my opinion, will definitely lead to an escalation of the conflict between the Fed and the White House. President Trump may view the Fed's latest action, or rather inaction, as an act of political competition in an interparty struggle where the Fed opposes Trump while taking the side of the Democrats. And Trump, I think, will not be able to keep quiet. So I think the escalation of the conflict is inevitable.

As a result, earlier I thought that the market may get after the Fed meeting a very positive fundamental factor in favor of market growth in the form of easing of the Fed policy. Now I believe that the market has actually received two strong negative factors. The first is the Fed policy itself, which will be restrained and will not give the economy money, and the second is the conflict between the Fed and Trump.

At the same time, I would note that since 2008, the main tool to combat the problems in the economy has been a soft monetary policy. This time, that tool is probably not going to be used anytime soon.

Other aspects

In addition, it is worth recalling that the data for the first quarter in the U.S. economy was negative. And this is already a fact. It is also worth considering that the trade war is a fact!

Although Trump announced a pause with the introduction of duties for 90 days, nevertheless, I think that eventually the duties will be raised even with all key countries sitting down at the negotiating table.

It didn't make sense on Trump's part to announce duties only to then eliminate them all completely. I think Trump imposed large duties in order to discount them later in the negotiations, but in the end, some amount of duties will eventually be left. So, on the situation with the duties, you have to realize that 100% of the situation will not be rolled back as it was in February of this year. The only question is what the size of the duties will be in the end. And in this context, we should not expect that the upcoming negotiations between the U.S. and China will not cancel trade barriers as before.

To summarize, from a fundamental point of view, compared to February. We have additional negatives in the form of the customs war, weak economic data for the first quarter, the unwillingness of the Fed to help the economy, and a potential conflict between the Fed and Donald Trump. That being said, as I believe, the bulls simply do not have strong fundamentals to play for market growth.

Technical Analysis

If we try to match the fundamental data with technical analysis, a 50% Fibonacci rebound from the downside looks sufficient in my opinion. I don't see any reason for a stronger bounce.

In the picture below, I have drawn the way the waves on the market according to Eliot.

From the end of February to the beginning of April, we had a falling wave A, then it was replaced by wave B and a bounce at the 50% Fibonacci level. And now, since I believe that the fundamentals are now on the bearish side, I expect a third wave C down.

If we try to connect these waves with the fundamental components, we will get the following picture.

Wave A - The market fall was due to the imposition of duties by Donald Trump, which was the main factor. In detail, the technical realization occurred according to the FLAG pattern. First, the duties were announced, and the market fell to the 5500 levels of the S&P500, then there was a short waiting period and a small rebound, and then the duties were implemented. As a result, the next wave of decline followed.

Wave B - After a fall, there is an oversold effect anyway, and many investors try to buy the cheapened assets. But in our situation, the rebound was boosted by hopes that the Fed might help; the market, in particular, increased its expectations for the number of Fed rate cuts this year. And most importantly, the biggest market move came after Trump announced a 90-day pause on tariff hikes.

Wave C - Summary

But as I wrote in the first part of this article, the Fed recently took away the hope of easing its policy soon. And the Trade War is not fiction, but reality. The 90-day delay doesn't eliminate tariffs, it only gives pause. And in the end, I think the duties will still be imposed, perhaps in a smaller amount, but nevertheless, Trump raised the duties not to eliminate them, but to increase them. And plus, we have an economy that is showing negative signals. So I think the market is well-positioned for a C wave to materialize. But of course, it is hard to say to what bottom this wave C will be.

In a favorable scenario, the bottom of wave C may coincide with the bottom of wave A. And in general, then we will get a double bottom, after which we can again talk about growth. This scenario is quite probable, but it is not excluded that the market may fall further. For now, I will not estimate where the market bottom will be.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Strong Growth Where It Counts Will Push Shares Higher
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $457.5 billion as of this writing, Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) is not only a massive energy play. It's one of the largest publicly traded companies on the planet. This is especially true if we exclude technology firms from the equation. In the past, the company has seen mixed financial performance. This is to be expected for a business that is heavily exposed to wild price swings in the commodities that it deals with. However, management has demonstrated time and time again that they are on the right path toward achieving their objectives. While financial performance this year is looking mixed so far, the business continues to grow its physical presence. And that is why I have been consistently bullish about it.

Recently, that bullishness has paid off. Since I last wrote about the firm in an article published in December of last year, an article in which I reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate, shares have seen downside of 0.6%. That is better than the 2.9% drop that the broader market experienced. Right now, I would say that there is heightened uncertainty because of worries about energy prices. This uncertainty stems from broader economic worries caused by the current trade war situation, and the fact that OPEC+ is continuing to raise oil output at a rate that is faster than what had previously been expected. Near term, this could certainly negatively affect the business. But considering how shares are priced on an absolute basis, and considering the path that management has the business on, I believe that maintaining Exxon Mobil as a soft ‘buy’ is appropriate.

Staying the course

Perhaps the best way to start off our analysis of Exxon Mobil is to look at the company’s most recent financial performance. Year over year for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, we can see that the company achieved revenue of $83.13 billion. That is slightly above the $83.08 billion reported the previous year. Even though revenue ticked up very slightly, profitability has been a bit volatile. Net income, for instance, contracted from $8.22 billion to $7.71 billion. There weren't any special one time things experienced in either of those quarters. Examples here would be impairments, gains or losses on the sale of assets, and certain tax related items. So adjusted net profits ended up coming in the same as GAAP profits did.

Cash flows ended up being all over the place. Operating cash flow, for instance, contracted from $14.66 billion to $12.93 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an increase from $12.66 billion to $13.83 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company ticked up from $17.40 billion to $17.51 billion. All of these things taken in their entirety, I would say that the overall picture for the business was positive during this time.

While it's nice to look at the picture from a 30,000 foot view, I think it is imperative to dig down deeper to see what is going on beneath the hood. I say this because the mixed financial performance of the business might seem unimpressive. But when you dig deeper, things look quite solid. As an example, we need to only take the Upstream segment of the business. This is the part of the company that focuses on exploration and production activities. It's also the part of the firm that is perhaps the most volatile from year to year. To put this in perspective, during the first quarter of 2025, this segment for the business generated a net profit of $6.76 billion. This represents a pretty substantial increase over the $5.66 billion reported one year earlier.

This might be a little surprising to anybody who has seen energy prices as of late. I say this because, from the first quarter of the 2024 fiscal year to the first quarter of 2025, WTI crude prices fell from $77.06 per barrel to $71.46 per barrel. Natural gas prices rose from $2.25 per Mcf to $3.65 per Mcf. Similar year over year changes occurred with international oil and gas prices, with the company similarly seeing declines in realized prices. But it wasn't just the natural gas increase that helped the company. It was also the fact that the business is producing more oil and gas now than it was a year ago. Net production of crude oil, NGLs, bitumen, and synthetic oil, hit 3.14 million boe (barrels of oil equivalent) per day in the first quarter of 2025. That's 22.8% above the 2.56 million reported the previous year. Natural gas production, meanwhile, jumped 15.1% from 1.23 million boe per day to 1.41 million boe per day on a year over year basis.

Even as the company saw a hit because of its decision to sell off non-core assets, it benefited from a growth perspective from its acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources, which cost shareholders $63 billion in shares and another $5 billion in net debt assumption. The firm also benefited from the $4.99 billion in capital expenditure projects that it focused on in the first quarter of 2025. These were only the capital projects associated with its Upstream business, and they accounted for 84.1% of the $5.94 billion spent during the quarter. The spending associated with this segment represented an increase of 21.6% compared to the $4.11 billion reported to the previous year.

There are naturally some other segments as well. The Energy Products part of the company, for instance, saw a drop in profits from $1.38 billion to $827 million on a year over year basis. This was in spite of the fact that overall product sales under this segment increased marginally from 5.23 million boe per day to 5.28 million boe per day. Management claimed that this was the result of weaker industry refining margins that were offset only slightly by major cost savings initiatives, foreign currency fluctuations, and certain timing effects that management did not go much into. According to one source, there is hope that refining margins could see a recovery this year. This would be because of plant closures and a heavy spring maintenance season. If everything goes according to plan, these could increase crack spreads later on in 2025. In terms of closures, it's estimated that as much as 800,000 barrels per day of global refining capacity could be shut down. But with this tariff situation, uncertainty is significant.

Other parts of the company have experienced weakness also. The Chemical Products segment for the company, for instance, saw profits dropped from $785 million to $273 million. Management attributed this to weaker industry margins, a reduction in sales volume from 5.05 million tons to 4.78 million tons, and to higher expenses as the company works to turn around its operations in this segment and invests in startup costs for certain projects. There is some hope that overall output for this segment will improve. This is because, during the first quarter, the company commenced operations ahead of schedule and under budget at its China Chemical Complex that, when fully operational, will be able to produce up to 1.7 million tons per year of polyethylene and 850,000 tons per year of polypropylene. But it will take the rest of this year for production to ramp up. This unit is also benefiting from an advanced recycling unit in Baytown that commenced operations in April and that is capable of processing 80 million pounds annually of plastic waste. This is part of the company's decision to invest more heavily in renewable projects and is doubling its existing advantage to recycling capacity.

Lastly, we have the Specialty Products segment of the business. Year over year in the first quarter, there was a slight decline in sales from 1.96 million tons to 1.94 million tons. But profits dropped rather meaningfully from $761 million to $655 million. Management claimed that this was because of additional structural cost savings being more than offset by higher costs because of new market developments and foreign currency fluctuations that negatively affected the business. On top of all of this, corporate costs for the business increased such that the loss on the corporate side more than doubled from $362 million to $798 million. Again, foreign currency fluctuations played a role here. But so did pension related costs and lower interest income.

It is clear, looking at the picture, that while Exxon Mobil is most certainly a diverse player in the energy space, what you would call an integrated energy play, its big emphasis moving forward is on its Upstream operation. Thanks to the aforementioned acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources, as well as additional planned spending on capital projects, the company hopes to boost overall output from 4.3 million boe per day in 2024 to 5.4 million boe per day in 2030.

During this time, management's emphasis is on not only increasing production, but also on increasing profits from advantaged projects and growing high value product sales. It hopes that, by 2030, it will be generating around $1.5 billion in profits, on an average quarterly basis, from these initiatives. That's up from only $460 million that the company generated in the first quarter of 2025. A lot of the boost in profitability should also come from additional structural cost savings that the business is focused on. From 2019 to 2024, cumulative structural cost savings that the company has achieved came out to $12.1 billion on an annualized basis. That number increased further to $12.7 billion in the first quarter of this year. By 2030, management expects this to increase even more to $18 billion.

In my most recent article about the company, I dug deep into management's plans over the next several years. Given how little the share price has changed and the fact that management has not revised, meaningfully, any of its expectations for its forecast, I think it would just be better to recommend that you read that article after finishing this one. However, it is obvious to me that the $2.3 trillion in potential addressable markets that exist between now and 2050 remain attractive for the business and the firm is well positioned to capitalize on these prospects.

Even if we just focus on the financial performance that the business has achieved recently, shares look attractively priced to me. In the chart above, you can see that the company is trading in the mid to high single digit range relative to cash flows. This is based on historical figures from both 2023 and 2024. Relative to other similar companies, which can be seen in the table below, the stock is certainly on the pricey side of the spectrum. On a price to earnings basis, three of the five businesses ended up being cheaper than Exxon Mobil is. On a price to operating cash flow basis, it ended up being tied as the most expensive of the group. And when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, we can see that it ended up being the clear most expensive prospect. But again, none of this factors in the continued growth of the company expects and the fact that this growth is already underway as evidenced by the data throughout this article.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Exxon Mobil Corporation 13.7 8.0 6.6
Chevron (CVX) 14.3 8.0 5.8
Shell (SHEL) 13.3 4.0 3.6
TotalEnergies SE (TTE) 9.7 4.3 3.9
BP plc (BP) 411.9 3.0 4.3
Suncor Energy (SU) 10.5 4.0 4.2

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I think that the market has underestimated Exxon Mobil. The company is a solid operator in its space and continues to achieve impressive results because of big investments that management is making. The firm has a concrete plan and evidence that it has succeeded recently in adhering to its growth and cost cutting initiatives. When you add all of this together, I believe that this makes the company a good soft 'buy’ candidate.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: Cannabis Meltdown Continues - Rich Dividends For The Opportunistic
data.description:
NLCP Remains Compelling As A Dividend Story, Assuming No Further Tenant Issues

We previously covered NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP) in March 2025, discussing why the sell-off had triggered an excellent buying opportunity for those seeking rich return profiles, attributed to the overly discounted valuations.

Combined with the higher upside potential from current levels, significantly made sweeter by the highly compelling forward dividend yields of 11.51% and the healthy balance sheet with low debt, we had rated the stock as a Strong Buy then.

NLCP 1Y Stock Price

Since then, NLCP has already further declined by -4.1%, with a similar underperformance also observed in its cannabis REIT peers.

Much of their headwinds are attributed to their numerous tenant default issues, along with the pessimism observed in the wider legal cannabis market and the reduced likelihood of a cannabis federal legalization during the new US administration over the next four years.

For reference, licensed cannabis operators face intense competition from illicit market players, as some New York dispensaries also sell "from unauthorized sources or is grown out of state, an illegal practice that has been called the industry’s open secret."

These developments have naturally contributed to the legal cannabis operators' impacted performance, attributed to their razor-thin profit margins in a challenging macroeconomic environment, worsened by the industry's lack of legal banking access.

Combined with the impacted consumer discretionary spending, it is unsurprising that NLCP's peer, Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), has already reported numerous rental collection issues, as four of its tenants default on their rents by March 2025 with 27.8% of its revenues at risk.

These issues have already contributed to the REIT's impacted FQ4'24 and FQ1'25 performance, as observed in the negative rental revenues and AFFO per share growth on a YoY basis.

The same tenant issue has been reported by NLCP, as Revolutionary Clinics and Calypso Enterprises report operating challenges from H2'24 and fail to pay rent as per contract.

With these two tenants comprising 12.8% (-0.7 points YoY) of the REIT's FY2024 annualized rental revenues and 13.5% of its FY2023 revenues, it is unsurprising that the market has discounted its prospects, since more tenant defaults may occur ahead.

IIPR's & NLCP's Tenant List

Even so, while both IIPR and NLCP may share a somewhat similar tenant list as shown above, with red indicating troubled tenants with recent rental defaults and green indicating Multi-State Operators with stable financial metrics, it is apparent that NLCP has had a drastically smaller exposure to those with troubled cash flows.

This implies that NLCP may be somewhat better positioned than IIPR, since the former continues to report a relatively stable FQ1'25 rental incomes of $12.58M (+2.5% QoQ/+3.7% YoY) and AFFO per share of $0.51 (-1.9% QoQ/-1.9% YoY), aided by the 98% of rental collection.

While NLCP may have a very limited number of tenants, with the top five tenants already comprising 62.4% of its FY2024 annualized rental revenues (+5.3 points YoY), readers may want to note that most of these tenants continue to report decent top/bottom-lines in their recent earnings call.

For example, Curaleaf (CURA:CA) (OTCPK:CURLF), which commands 22.8% of NLCP's revenues, has reported stable performance metrics in FQ4'24 while consistently growing its footprints and expanding its product lines into hemp derived THC seltzers.

The similarly promising developments have also been reported by Cresco Labs (CSE:CL) (OTCQX:CRLBF) (FSE:6CQ) and Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TRUL) (TCNNF), aside from Cannabist, which is undergoing footprint/operation optimization and C3 Industries, not publicly listed.

These imply that IIPR ongoing tenants' headwinds may not be directly translated to all legal cannabis operators, with NLCP's recent stock price correction merely attributed to an instance of when "a falling tide sinking all ships."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain somewhat optimistic, with NLCP expected to report a somewhat stable top/bottom-line performance over the next two years.

Assuming no further tenant troubles, the cannabis REIT may be able to continue paying out rich dividends as well, despite the increasingly elevated FQ1'25 AFFO payout ratio at 84% (+1 points QoQ/+5.7% YoY).

NLCP Valuations

These reasons are also why NLCP remains compelling at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 7.54x, down from the 1Y mean of 9.28x and 4Y mean of 9.91x.

The same discount may also be observed in IIPR at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 7.36x, compared to its 1Y mean of 10.92x and 5Y mean of 14.97x, as the market's pessimism surrounding the prospects of the cannabis operators and cannabis REIT markets in general triggers opportunistic entry points for value oriented investors.

Risk Warning

Nonetheless, it goes without saying that investing here carries notable risks, aside from the challenging market condition.

This is especially since the new US administration's intensified tariff war may trigger a higher cost of production, given the cannabis industry's reliance on a global supply chain for "essential equipment to grow cannabis, machinery, and accessories—everything from vaporizers to packaging and cartridges," including China and Southeast Asian countries.

For now, Justin Tacy, the Vice President of Marketing at PAX, a cannabis brand selling both cannabis products and vaporizers, has already highlighted that "high cannabis taxes, combined with tariffs, raise costs and may push buyers to the illicit market or force producers to cut costs, potentially compromising quality."

This is worsened by the potentially driven "up prices, pushing consumers to the illicit market and forcing small businesses to close because they can’t absorb the costs, leading to industry consolidation that could stifle innovation and reduce competition," as speculated by Michael Brosgart, president of cannabis vaping company Active.

With an end to end US manufacturing yet available while potentially being economically inefficient, it is unsurprising why NLCP and IIPR have been corrected as they have after the 2024 stock price heights, with it remaining to be seen if they are able to weather the intermediate term uncertainties.

So, Is NLCP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

NLCP 3Y Stock Price

For now, NLCP has already lost -32.7% or the equivalent -$139.61M of its market capitalization from the 52-week heights, otherwise, -53.9% or -$376.04M from the all-time heights, respectively, as the cannabis industry faces mounting headwinds.

On the one hand, readers must note that the meltdown has already triggered its richer forward dividend yields of 11.91%, compared to the 4Y averages of 7.80% and the REIT sector median of 4.36%, with its pay-outs remaining well covered by the ongoing operations, assuming no further tenant issues.

If anything, we must highlight that IIPR has made the decision to maintain its quarterly dividend payout sum in April 2025, despite the recent tenant headwinds and the FQ1'25 AFFO per share decline to $1.94 (-12.6% QoQ/-12.2% YoY) in FQ1'25.

On the other hand, it remains to be seen when the overall cannabis headwinds may end, as discussed in the risk section above, since IIPR's decision to continue paying dividends has already triggered a very risky and higher AFFO payout ratio of 97% (+11 points QoQ/+15 YoY).

This is on top of the higher net debt levels of -$161.48M (-6.5% QoQ/-12.6% YoY), with the REIT likely to face a deteriorating balance sheet if they do not cut dividends and/or its rental collection does not improve.

As a result, while we may cautiously rate the NLCP stock as a speculative Buy here, attributed to the rich dividend investment thesis, it goes without saying that our rating is only suitable for those with higher risk tolerance, since it is uncertain if the REIT's tenants may be able to be an exception to the ongoing rental defaults.

This stock may not be for the faint-hearted indeed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mister Car Wash: Various Near-Term Headwinds Limit Upside Potential
data.description:
Investment action

I give a hold rating for Mister Car Wash (MCW) because while MCW continues to execute well operationally, the uncertain macro backdrop and its potential impact on consumer discretionary spending are significant near-term risks that may hurt MCW's near-term earnings outlook. My stance is to wait for more clarity before turning bullish.

Company description

MCW is a car wash chain in the US with around 518 locations as of 1Q25. The main services it provides are express exterior and interior cleaning. It has a membership model called Unlimited Wash Club [UWC] that currently has >2.2 million members. The model charges members a monthly fee in exchange for unlimited washes and dedicated lane access. As of 1Q25, the revenue split between UWC sales and normal retail sales is 73% vs. 27%.

1Q25 Earnings Review

MCW reported 9.4% y/y 1Q25 revenue growth with a solid 6% growth in comparable store sales [CSS]. Demand was steady across both UWC and retail. UWC grew membership by 5% y/y to >2.2 million, and total UWC revenue grew 8.8% y/y. As for retail sales, revenue grew 10.9% y/y. Profitability also moved in the right direction. Adj. EBITDA grew 13.9% to $85.6 million, with margins expanding 130 bps to 32.7%. As a result, net income came in at $35 million, or $0.11 per share. MCW also delevered its balance sheet by voluntarily paying down $62 million in debt during the quarter. The current expectation is for net leverage to go just below 2.5x EBITDA by year-end.

Pricing Power, Good Cost Control, and Expanding Store Base

One of the more important developments for MCW is its first-ever price increase for the base UWC tier. The monthly fee is moving from $19.99 to around $22.99 in most markets, impacting ~40% of the member base. The pace of rollout is expected to be completed by May or June (across 5 regions), as there is a 30–60 day delay to notify members. If this plays out well, this move could meaningfully lift average revenue per member and drive top-line growth. Simple math would mean a ~6% increase to the average selling price for base tier members, or a 6% uplift to UWC revenue directly, assuming no churn. Importantly, management noted that pricing across the industry is becoming more rational, which should continue to give MCW room to raise prices. Complementing MCW’s pricing power is solid cost control, which can be seen from the reduction in labor and chemical costs as a percentage of revenue—down 160 bps—and G&A down 120 bps. In my view, this combination is a strong setup for margin to continue expanding if the churn stays manageable and macro conditions remain stable.

Aside from pricing, MCW also has another growth lever—store expansion—and they have remained aggressive on this front. In 1Q25, MCW opened four new greenfield locations, and management is sticking to their FY25 target of 30 to 35 new stores, implying a sequential ramp-up in the coming quarters. The long-term goal also remains intact—to double the current US footprint organically.

I believe this expansion is plausible because of the easing competitive landscape. MCW noted just 7 new competitor stores opening within a 3-mile radius in 1Q25, down from 15 last year and 33 the year before. This is a stark contrast to MCW's expansion record—opening 33 and 38 in FY24/23, respectively. The way I see it, it shows that the industry is consolidating, and MCW is taking advantage to capture more real estate space. Encouragingly, even in areas where competition does show up, management noted that stores facing new rivals tend to recover and outperform the chain average within 18 to 24 months.

Potential Headwinds

Now, I don’t think this is the right time to invest in MCW is because of a few potential headwinds that could hurt the business in the near term.

Firstly, while the UWC price hike should lift revenue, I think it is better to monitor the churn impact before turning bullish. Despite the encouraging results from test markets, management did acknowledge an “initial increase in churn” from this move. Also, remember that consumers in test markets may not be representative of consumers in other regions. If cancellations come in more than expected—especially in a weaker macro environment—it could offset the benefit of higher pricing and weigh on net membership growth.

Secondly, and this is perhaps the more concerning reason, UWC growth is ultimately tightly linked to retail traffic. Management called out that 5% member growth in 1Q25 tracked almost exactly with a 5% lift in retail visits. That makes the retail channel a key driver—and a major risk as well. If retail traffic softens, subscription growth could slow down as well. And given the macro backdrop, I don’t think this is an unlikely scenario. The US economy has already shown signs of weakness, including a GDP contraction in 1Q25 and persistent inflation pressure. Consumers are clearly feeling it, as indicated by the drop in the consumer confidence index. This is very important because pay-per-wash visits are likely among the first to be cut when consumers tighten their budget. And if those visits decline, it directly shrinks the pool of potential new UWC sign-ups.

Valuation

On the valuation front, I wouldn’t jump to say that MCW is cheap just because it is at the low end of its historical trading band—16x NTM PE. I think the market is right to value it at the current price because things could go both ways. If the US economy holds up better than expected, MCW will have no issues pushing through price increases and staying on its aggressive store expansion strategy. However, if the economy gets worse—consumers further tighten their budget—we could see a sharp drop in car wash demand (consumers can just wash themselves to save cost), and this directly impacts UWC growth. In the latter scenario, earnings growth is likely to be negative, and I do not want to be invested when that happens. As such, I think the current multiple is fair. This does not mean that MCW is uninvestable. I think the right timing to invest is when we see a few things: (1) UWC price increases face no churn; (2) the US economy gradually gets better; and (3) no step up in competitive pressure. All of these should become clearer in the coming quarters.

Conclusion

I am giving a hold rating on MCW for now. The near-term setup is a bit murky, and there is real execution risk around the UWC price increase, especially if churn runs higher than expected. And with retail traffic so tightly linked to subscription growth, any slowdown from a cautious consumer could weigh on growth momentum. Until there’s more clarity on how these factors play out, I think it makes sense to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC Trades At 78 Cents On A Dollar, But Is The 'Dollar' Stable?
data.description:
Goldman Sachs BDC (NYSE:GSBD) has had a rough ride over the past year and a half. For example, since my first bearish article on GSBD that was published back in July 2024, the BDC has lost more than one-third of its market cap. As I expected, earlier this year, GSBD announced a dividend cut, which has not so far changed the situation to the positive side. In my Q4, 2024 earnings report review piece, I still outlined a skeptical thesis, arguing that the chances were higher for a further downside than a sudden recovery. Since then, the total return result has been negative, at ~15%.

Now, GSBD has circulated its Q1, 2025 earnings figures, which provide a fresh base of information for us to contextualize with the current dividend yield of ~12.2% and a P/NAV of 0.78x (i.e., discount of 22%).

The entry point in GSBD looks optically very attractive.

The question is whether the dividend is sustainable and whether the existing discount has already baked into the cake all of the known and some part of the currently unknown risks.

Let's try to answer this together by dissecting the most recent data points.

Thesis review

As the title indicates, what we have to really understand is whether the NAV or the “dollar” is stable enough that paying “78 cents” for it would make it a bargain. The sustainability of the dividend yield is related but a secondary aspect. If the NAV can remain stable or even grow without the help of a potentially reduced dividend, then we have a beautiful story here for tangible current income and very likely price appreciation.

Looking at the Q1, 2025 report, my immediate reaction is that GSBD has still failed to stabilize its operations.

In Q1, 2025, the adjusted NII per share decreased to $0.41 from $0.47 per share in the prior quarter. The drop was driven by a significant reduction in the interest income (due to lower portfolio yield) and increasing interest expense.

While the $0.41 per share result was sufficient to cover the base dividend of $0.32 per share, if we consider the supplemental distribution and unrealized losses, the generated adjusted NII was still not enough to avoid the NAV decline. Namely, the NAV per share came in at $13.20—a notable drop relative to the Q4, 2024 statistic of $13.41 per share.

There are two concerns in this context. One is adjusted NII per share related, and the other one is net unrealized/realized loss related.

The first aspect has two sides. The positive is that Q1, 2025, already includes all of the lagged effects from the lower base rates. Plus, the amortized portfolio yield sits at 10%, which is not a significant difference from where the market financing levels are. So, headwinds from the yield compression dynamics are likely to be over or remain manageable.

On the negative side, I have to mention these factors that could drive the figure lower going forward:

  • 2026 fixed unsecured note refinancing that has a yield of just 2.87%. Clearly, the new loan will cost more.
  • Lower asset base from negative net funding volume in Q1, 2025.
  • Incremental non-accruals, which we have to factor into our base case.

However, the base dividend coverage is at 128%, which provides a decent cushion for additional shock absorption.

The second aspect is a bit bigger concern. It is the key reason why the NAV has declined. And looking back at the past 4 financial quarters, we will see that in each quarter there has been a struggle for GSBD to avoid a net negative result from the unrealized/realized gain component. In this quarter, the hit came from the assumption pertaining to the portfolio company's potential challenges that could be induced by the tariffs. It is very hard to predict how this specific component will evolve going forward. The recent history is not on the GSBD investor side. Furthermore, I would say that the portfolio-wide company credit statistics do not send an encouraging signal either:

  • Weighted average interest coverage is 1.9x, which is not bad, but not good either. For example, my favorite BDC – Kayne Anderson (NYSE:KBDC) – has interest coverage above 3x.
  • Weighted average leverage (net debt-to-EBITDA) is 5.8x, which is clearly skewed towards the more aggressive side.

Finally, if we speak about growth, then I do not see an overly positive outlook for GSBD. The debt-to-equity is at 1.16x, which is within a typical BDC sweet spot range. Going above that would be just too aggressive, given the underlying risks and/or exposures towards potentially weak portfolio companies. Plus, the capital/M&A market itself is frozen, which makes growth very difficult.

The bottom line

In a nutshell, I think that GSBD's NAV is far from being stable. Or let me rephrase that. While it is not subject to very steep and even dangerous losses, the odds are still stacked in favor of seeing further NAV reductions, at least over the next couple of quarters. The net unrealized/realized loss momentum, weak portfolio company credit statistics, and unfavorable market environment do not particularly help to maintain the NAV unchanged.

The good thing, however, is that GSBD's dividend coverage of 128% leaves a solid amount of undistributed income in the system that can act as a shield from NAV-negative items.

At the end of the day, we have to decide what level of risk we are willing to take. GSBD is definitely a high-risk play, but with not that bad risk-to-reward ratio. Also, the downside is not totally unprotected.

I would not invest in GSBD for stable total returns. While the dividend looks safe, the NAV is vulnerable and could trigger tangible price declines.

If I decided to invest in GSBD, then it would be a minor position with an expectation that, in case the markets recover, GSBD should lead to outsized alpha performance. However, I am not planning to do so, because of CION Investment Corporation (NYSE:CION), which, in my view, embodies a greater risk-to-reward ratio as a speculative investment.
